text: Here we find the inferior pulmonary ligament.

and is being handed over to the assistant.

which here looks very large and rather malignant, so this was removed.

It is being dissected out with the aid of the cadier and the Maryland forceps to create a passage underneath for the stapler.
Here you can see a leader catheter being brought in.
It is attached to a gold curved tip 1045 stapler brought in by the assistant through the utility port.
Mr. Dunning guides the leader catheter underneath the lingular artery using the cat ear, which is quite nicely being used to thread the leader catheter underneath the lingular artery.
This is then followed by the stapler.
As you can see, the leader catheter makes it much easier to guide a junior user of the stapler across this delicate and small vessel.
The unique curved tip of the stapler also makes this easier.
Having the third arm to the robot is really useful and here you can see the lung graspers operated by the third arm retracting the lung to carefully enable stapling of the vessel.
The lingular artery is then divided and here you can see the stump.
Now we reach the lingular vein, which is being dissected using the cat ear and Maryland forceps to create a passage for the stapler.
As you can see, the cat ear is really great for blunt dissection, whilst the Maryland diathermy is great for focused and dry cuts.
Here, Mr. Dunning is just dissecting around the lingular vein, and it looks like he's come across some lymph nodes.

is used to divide the lingular vein.
